question: The Railways Act, 1989 (Act No. 24 of 1989) received the assent of the President on:
answer: 3rd June 1989
explanation: The Railways Act, 1989 received Presidential assent on 3rd June 1989. It came into force on 1st July 1990 vide notification S.O. 475(E) dated 12th June 1990.

question: The Railways Act, 1989 came into force on:
answer: 1st July 1990
explanation: Though assented on 3rd June 1989, the Act came into force on 1st July 1990 vide notification S.O. 475(E) dated 12th June 1990.

question: The Railways Act, 1989 replaced which earlier legislation?
answer: Indian Railways Act, 1890
explanation: Section 200 of the Railways Act, 1989 repeals the Indian Railways Act, 1890 (9 of 1890).

question: Under the Railways Act 1989, 'demurrage' means:
options: Charge levied for detention of rolling stock after expiry of free time
answer: Charge levied for detention of rolling stock after expiry of free time
explanation: Section 2(11) defines 'demurrage' as the charge levied for the detention of any rolling stock after the expiry of free time, if any, allowed for such detention.

question: Under the Railways Act 1989, 'wharfage' means:
options: Charge levied on goods for not removing them from the railway after expiry of free time
Charge for storage of goods in railway warehouses
Penalty for overstaying at railway premises
answer: Charge levied on goods for not removing them from the railway after expiry of free time
explanation: Section 2(41) defines 'wharfage' as the charge levied on goods for not removing them from the railway after the expiry of the free time for such removal.

question: Under the Railways Act 1989, a 'lump sum rate' means:
options: Rate fixed for a class of commodity
Rate mutually agreed between railway administration and a consignor
Special reduced rate for specific commodity between specified stations
Rate fixed by the Railway Rates Tribunal
answer: Rate mutually agreed between railway administration and a consignor
explanation: Section 2(24) defines 'lump sum rate' as the rate mutually agreed upon between a railway administration and a consignor for the carriage of goods and for any service in relation to such carriage.

question: Under Section 2(14), 'fare' means:
options: Charge levied for the carriage of goods
Charge levied for the carriage of passengers
Any charge including freight and demurrage
Charge fixed by the Railway Rates Tribunal
answer: Charge levied for the carriage of passengers
explanation: Section 2(14) specifically defines 'fare' as the charge levied for the carriage of passengers — distinguishing it from 'freight' which applies to goods.

question: Under the Railways Act 1989, 'rolling stock' includes:
options: Locomotives, tenders, carriages, wagons, rail-cars, containers, trucks, trolleys and all vehicles moving on rails
Only goods wagons and passenger coaches
Locomotives, carriages and track maintenance vehicles
answer: Locomotives, tenders, carriages, wagons, rail-cars, containers, trucks, trolleys and all vehicles moving on rails
explanation: Section 2(37) gives a comprehensive definition of rolling stock including all vehicles moving on rails.

question: The definition of 'railway land' was inserted into the Railways Act 1989 by:
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2005
explanation: Section 2(32A) defining 'railway land' as any land in which a Government railway has any right, title or interest was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2005 (Act 47 of 2005), w.e.f. 30-8-2006.

question: The definition of 'Board' (Railway Board) was inserted into the Railways Act 1989 by:
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2025
explanation: Section 2(1B) defining 'Board' as the Railway Board constituted under section 2A was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2025 (Act 9 of 2025), w.e.f. 20-8-2025.

question: The definition of 'officer authorised' was inserted into Section 2 of the Railways Act 1989 by:
answer: Railways (Second Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 51 of 2003)
explanation: Section 2(26A) defining 'officer authorised' was inserted by Act 51 of 2003, w.e.f. 1-7-2004. The officer authorised means an officer authorised by the Central Government under sub-section (2) of section 179.

question: Chapter IA dealing with 'Railway Board' was inserted into the Railways Act 1989 by which amendment?
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2025
explanation: Chapter IA containing sections 2A and 2B on the Railway Board was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2025 (Act 9 of 2025), w.e.f. 20-8-2025.

question: Under Section 2A, the Railway Board constituted under the Resolution of Government of India, PWD No. 256G of 18th February 1905 shall be deemed to be:
options: The Railway Board constituted under this Act
A temporary body pending fresh constitution
An advisory body to the Ministry of Railways
answer: The Railway Board constituted under this Act
explanation: Section 2A(1) provides that the Railway Board constituted under the 1905 Resolution, with its composition as revised from time to time, shall be deemed to be the Railway Board constituted under the Railways Act, 1989.

question: Under Section 2B, communications from the Railway Board are binding if:
options: Approved by the Ministry of Railways
In writing signed by the Secretary to the Board or any authorised person
Approved by a majority of Board members in a meeting
answer: In writing signed by the Secretary to the Board or any authorised person
explanation: Section 2B provides that communications shall be binding if in writing signed by the Secretary to the Board, or by any other person authorised by the Board to act in its behalf.

question: The Central Government may constitute Zonal Railways by:
answer: Notification in the Official Gazette
explanation: Section 3(1) provides that the Central Government may, for efficient administration of Government railways, by notification, constitute such railways into Zonal Railways.

question: The general superintendence and control of a Zonal Railway vests in:
answer: The General Manager
explanation: Section 4(2) provides that the general superintendence and control of a Zonal Railway shall vest in the General Manager.

question: Chapter IIA on 'Rail Land Development Authority' was inserted into the Railways Act 1989 by:
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2005
chapter: Chapter IIA — Rail Land Development Authority
explanation: Chapter IIA containing sections 4A to 4I on the RLDA was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2005 (Act 47 of 2005), w.e.f. 30-8-2006.

question: The Chairman of the Rail Land Development Authority (RLDA) is, ex officio:
answer: Member Engineering, Railway Board
chapter: Chapter IIA — Rail Land Development Authority
explanation: Section 4B(2) provides that the Member Engineering, Railway Board shall be the Chairman, ex officio, of the RLDA.

question: The RLDA consists of a Chairman, Vice-Chairman and how many other members?
answer: Not more than four
chapter: Chapter IIA — Rail Land Development Authority
explanation: Section 4B(1) provides that the Authority shall consist of a Chairman, Vice-Chairman and not more than four other members.

question: The salaries and expenses of the RLDA are defrayed from:
answer: Consolidated Fund of India
chapter: Chapter IIA — Rail Land Development Authority
explanation: Section 4H provides that the salaries, allowances and pensions payable to officers and employees of the RLDA shall be defrayed out of the Consolidated Fund of India.

question: The Commissioner of Railway Safety is deemed to be a public servant within the meaning of:
options: Section 2 of the Prevention of Corruption Act
Section 21 of the Indian Penal Code
Section 7 of the Prevention of Corruption Act
answer: Section 21 of the Indian Penal Code
chapter: Chapter III — Commissioners of Railway Safety
explanation: Section 8 of the Railways Act 1989 provides that the Commissioner shall be deemed to be a public servant within the meaning of section 21 of the Indian Penal Code.

question: The annual report of the Commissioners of Railway Safety is prepared by:
options: The Chief Commissioner of Railway Safety
answer: The Chief Commissioner of Railway Safety
chapter: Chapter III — Commissioners of Railway Safety
explanation: Section 10 provides that the Chief Commissioner of Railway Safety shall prepare in each financial year an annual report giving a full account of the activities of the Commissioners.

question: Under Section 22, before a railway is opened for public carriage of passengers, all of the following are required in the Commissioner's report EXCEPT:
options: Careful inspection of the railway and rolling stock
Moving and fixed dimensions have not been infringed
Adequate commercial revenue potential of the route
In his opinion the railway can be opened without danger to the public
answer: Adequate commercial revenue potential of the route
explanation: Section 22 requires the Commissioner's report to cover inspection, dimensions, structural compliance, and safety opinion — not commercial viability.

question: Under Section 24, after a traffic-suspending accident, the restored railway may be opened WITHOUT prior Commissioner inspection if:
options: The railway servant in charge certifies safety in writing AND notice is sent to Commissioner
The Ministry of Railways gives approval
The Divisional Railway Manager issues a safety certificate
answer: The railway servant in charge certifies safety in writing AND notice is sent to Commissioner
explanation: Section 24 permits opening without prior Commissioner inspection if: (a) the railway servant in charge certifies in writing that opening will not be attended with danger; and (b) a notice of opening is sent immediately to the Commissioner.

question: Under Section 33, the Railway Rates Tribunal consists of:
options: A Chairman and one other member
A Chairman and two other members
A Chairman and four other members
answer: A Chairman and two other members
explanation: Section 33(2) provides that the Tribunal shall consist of a Chairman and two other members to be appointed by the Central Government.

question: Who is qualified to be Chairman of the Railway Rates Tribunal?
options: Any serving IAS officer of Secretary rank
A person who is or has been a Judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court
The Chairman of the Railway Board
A retired General Manager of a Zonal Railway
answer: A person who is or has been a Judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court
explanation: Section 33(3) provides that a person shall not be qualified for appointment as Chairman of the Tribunal unless he is, or has been, a Judge of the Supreme Court or of a High Court.

question: The decisions/orders of the Railway Rates Tribunal are:
options: Subject to appeal before the High Court
Final, by majority of members sitting
Subject to approval by the Central Government
answer: Final, by majority of members sitting
explanation: Section 42 provides that the decisions or orders of the Tribunal shall be by a majority of the members sitting and shall be final.

question: Which of the following is NOT within the jurisdiction of the Railway Rates Tribunal?
options: Unreasonable freight rates between two stations
Undue preference in carriage of goods
Fixation of fares for carriage of passengers
answer: Fixation of fares for carriage of passengers
explanation: Section 37(c) explicitly excludes fixation of fares levied for carriage of passengers from the Tribunal's jurisdiction.

question: The definition of 'Tribunal' in Section 2(40) was substituted by:
answer: Finance Act, 2017
explanation: Section 2(40) was substituted by the Finance Act, 2017 (Act 7 of 2017), w.e.f. 26-5-2017. Sections 34 and 35 of the Railways Act were also omitted by the same Act.

question: Every railway administration shall display timetables and fare lists at stations in:
options: Hindi, English and the regional language of the area
answer: Hindi, English and the regional language of the area
explanation: Section 49(1) requires railway administrations to cause timetables and fare lists to be pasted in Hindi and English and also in the regional language commonly in use in the area.

question: Under Section 51, if a passenger travels in a lower class due to non-availability of his ticketed class, he is entitled to:
options: Refund of the difference between fare paid and fare for the class actually travelled
Compensation equal to double the difference
answer: Refund of the difference between fare paid and fare for the class actually travelled
explanation: Section 51(2) provides that if no accommodation is available in the ticketed class and the holder travels in a lower class, he shall be entitled to a refund of the difference between the fare paid and the fare payable for the class actually travelled.

question: Under Section 53, which ticket transfer IS permitted?
options: Mutual transfer of a seat or berth by passengers travelling by the same train
Transfer to a person of the same gender
Transfer with permission of Ticket Checking staff
answer: Mutual transfer of a seat or berth by passengers travelling by the same train
explanation: Section 53 provides that nothing shall prevent mutual transfer of a seat or berth by passengers travelling by the same train.

question: Under Section 58, every railway administration shall in every passenger train earmark for the exclusive use of females:
options: One compartment or such number of berths/seats as the railway administration thinks fit
answer: One compartment or such number of berths/seats as the railway administration thinks fit
explanation: Section 58 requires earmarking for the exclusive use of females one compartment or such number of berths or seats as the railway administration may think fit.

question: A forwarding note under Section 64 shall be executed by:
options: Every person entrusting goods to a railway administration for carriage
The railway servant at the destination station
Both the consignor and consignee jointly
answer: Every person entrusting goods to a railway administration for carriage
explanation: Section 64(1) requires that every person entrusting any goods to a railway administration for carriage shall execute a forwarding note in such form as may be specified by the Central Government.

question: A railway receipt under Section 65 is prima facie evidence of:
options: The weight and number of packages stated therein
Both weight and value of goods
answer: The weight and number of packages stated therein
explanation: Section 65(2) provides that a railway receipt shall be prima facie evidence of the weight and the number of packages stated therein.

question: Section 70 prohibits a railway administration from making or giving:
options: Any discount on freight for bulk consignments
Any undue or unreasonable preference or advantage to any particular person or description of traffic in carriage of goods
answer: Any undue or unreasonable preference or advantage to any particular person or description of traffic in carriage of goods
explanation: Section 70 is the anti-discrimination provision — prohibiting undue or unreasonable preference in the carriage of goods.

question: Under Section 73, the punitive charges for overloading beyond permissible capacity are recoverable by the railway administration:
answer: Before delivery of goods
explanation: Section 73 provides that charges by way of penalty shall be recovered at prescribed rates before the delivery of the goods.

question: Under Section 76, the railway administration shall deliver the consignment on:
options: Production of identity proof by the consignee
answer: Surrender of the railway receipt
explanation: Section 76 provides that the railway administration shall deliver the consignment on the surrender of the railway receipt.

question: For public auction of non-perishable detained consignments under Section 83, the railway administration shall give notice of not less than:
options: 7 days in one or more local newspapers
answer: 7 days in one or more local newspapers
explanation: Section 83(3) requires a notice of not less than seven days of the public auction in one or more local newspapers.

question: Goods at a notified station not removed within how many days after termination of transit may be sold by public auction?
answer: 7 days
chapter: Chapter X — Special Provisions for Notified Stations
explanation: Section 90(1) provides that goods at a notified station not removed within 7 days after the termination of transit may be sold by public auction.

question: A 'notified station' is declared by:
answer: The Central Government by notification
chapter: Chapter X — Special Provisions for Notified Stations
explanation: Section 89(1) provides that the Central Government may, if satisfied that goods should be removed without delay from a railway station, declare it to be a notified station.

question: The initial period for which a notified station notification remains in force shall not exceed:
answer: Six months
chapter: Chapter X — Special Provisions for Notified Stations
explanation: The second proviso to Section 89(1) provides that the period specified shall not exceed six months in the first instance, extendable by up to six months on each occasion.

question: Under Section 93, even where an excepted cause is proved, the railway administration is still liable if it fails to prove it used:
options: Reasonable foresight and care in the carriage of the goods
answer: Reasonable foresight and care in the carriage of the goods
explanation: The proviso to Section 93 requires the railway administration to prove not just the excepted cause, but also that it used reasonable foresight and care in the carriage of the goods.

question: Under Section 97, for goods at owner's risk rate, the railway administration is not responsible for loss UNLESS:
options: Negligence or misconduct on the part of the railway administration or its servants is proved
The consignor has declared the value
The destination station is in a different zone
answer: Negligence or misconduct on the part of the railway administration or its servants is proved
explanation: Section 97 provides that for goods at owner's risk rate, the railway administration shall not be responsible except upon proof of negligence or misconduct on its part or on the part of any of its servants.

question: After termination of transit, the railway administration remains responsible as a bailee for goods for:
answer: Seven days
explanation: Section 99(1) provides that the railway administration shall be responsible as a bailee under the Indian Contract Act for the loss, destruction, damage, deterioration or non-delivery of any consignment up to a period of seven days after the termination of transit.

question: The time limit for serving notice of claim for compensation for goods loss/damage is:
options: Three months from date of entrustment
Six months from date of entrustment
One year from date of entrustment
Ninety days from date of delivery
answer: Six months from date of entrustment
explanation: Section 106(1) provides that a person shall not be entitled to claim compensation unless a notice thereof is served within six months from the date of entrustment of the goods.

question: Under Section 113, after a railway accident the stationmaster shall give notice WITHOUT DELAY to:
options: The District Magistrate and Superintendent of Police within whose jurisdiction the accident occurs
Only the Commissioner of Railway Safety
answer: The District Magistrate and Superintendent of Police within whose jurisdiction the accident occurs
explanation: Section 113(1) requires the stationmaster to give notice without delay to the District Magistrate and Superintendent of Police, along with the police station officer and other appointed authorities.

question: Under Section 115, if the Commissioner is not able to hold an inquiry, the inquiry shall be conducted by:
options: The railway administration within whose jurisdiction the accident occurs
A Special Committee constituted by the Central Government
answer: The railway administration within whose jurisdiction the accident occurs
explanation: Section 115 provides that where the Commissioner does not hold inquiry or informs he is unable to, the railway administration within whose jurisdiction the accident occurs shall cause an inquiry to be made.

question: A statement made during an inquiry before the Commissioner under Section 117 shall NOT be used against the person EXCEPT:
options: In compensation claims by the victim's family
In a prosecution for giving false evidence by such statement
In any departmental inquiry against the railway servant concerned
In claims before the Railway Claims Tribunal
answer: In a prosecution for giving false evidence by such statement
explanation: Section 117 provides that no statement made before the Commissioner shall be used against the person except in a prosecution for giving false evidence by such statement.

question: Under Section 123, 'untoward incident' includes all EXCEPT:
options: Accidental falling of a passenger from the train
Derailment of a goods train with no passengers
answer: Derailment of a goods train with no passengers
explanation: Section 123(c) defines 'untoward incident' as: (a) terrorist act/act of violence against a passenger; (b) robbery or dacoity against a passenger; (c) accidental falling of a passenger from the train. A goods train derailment without passengers is not an untoward incident.

question: The liability under Section 124 for death or injury in a railway accident is on what basis?
options: Fault basis — negligence must be proved
No-fault basis — liability regardless of negligence
answer: No-fault basis — liability regardless of negligence
explanation: Section 124 clearly establishes no-fault liability — whether or not there has been any wrongful act, neglect or default on the part of the railway administration.

question: Section 124A on compensation for untoward incidents was inserted by:
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 56 of 2003)
explanation: Section 124A on compensation on account of untoward incidents was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 56 of 2003).

question: Under Section 124A, a passenger who has fallen from a train while travelling shall be deemed to have:
options: Been involved in a railway accident under section 124
Suffered injury for the purpose of claiming untoward incident compensation
No claim since it is not a railway accident
A claim only if the fall was due to overcrowding
answer: Suffered injury for the purpose of claiming untoward incident compensation
explanation: The Explanation to Section 124A specifically provides that a passenger who has fallen from a train while travelling shall be deemed to have suffered injury for compensation under that section.

question: Under Section 126, where a person desires interim relief pending determination by the Claims Tribunal, the application shall be made to:
options: The railway administration along with a copy of the Section 125 application
answer: The railway administration along with a copy of the Section 125 application
explanation: Section 126(1) provides that a person desiring interim relief may apply to the railway administration for payment of interim relief along with a copy of the application made under Section 125.

question: The maximum weekly working hours for a 'continuous' railway servant under Section 132 is:
options: 54 hours per week on average over two-weekly period
answer: 54 hours per week on average over two-weekly period
explanation: Section 132(2) provides that a railway servant whose employment is continuous shall not be employed for more than fifty-four hours a week on an average in a two-weekly period of fourteen days.

question: The maximum weekly working hours for an 'essentially intermittent' railway servant is:
answer: 75 hours per week
explanation: Section 132(1) provides that a railway servant whose employment is essentially intermittent shall not be employed for more than seventy-five hours in any week.

question: The maximum weekly working hours for an 'intensive' railway servant is:
options: 45 hours per week on average in two-weekly period
answer: 45 hours per week on average in two-weekly period
explanation: Section 132(3) provides that a railway servant whose employment is intensive shall not be employed for more than forty-five hours a week on an average in a two-weekly period of fourteen days.

question: Under Section 133, locomotive and traffic running staff shall be granted each month a rest of at least:
options: Two periods of not less than 30 consecutive hours each
Four periods of 30 consecutive hours OR five periods of 22 consecutive hours each, including a full night
Three periods of not less than 24 consecutive hours each
Six periods of not less than 12 consecutive hours each
answer: Four periods of 30 consecutive hours OR five periods of 22 consecutive hours each, including a full night
explanation: Section 133(2)(i) provides locomotive or traffic running staff shall be granted each month a rest of at least four periods of not less than thirty consecutive hours each or at least five periods of not less than twenty-two consecutive hours each, including a full night.

question: When overtime is required under Section 132(4) due to exceptional pressure of work, the overtime payment shall be at not less than:
options: Equal to ordinary rate of pay
One and a half times the ordinary rate of pay
Two times the ordinary rate of pay
Three times the ordinary rate of pay
answer: Two times the ordinary rate of pay
explanation: The proviso to Section 132(4) provides that overtime shall be paid at not less than two times the ordinary rate of pay for the excess hours.

question: Under Section 137, the minimum punishment for fraudulently travelling without a ticket (in the absence of special reasons) is:
answer: Fine of Rs. 500
explanation: Section 137 provides that in the absence of special and adequate reasons to the contrary, the punishment shall not be less than a fine of five hundred rupees.

question: Under Section 138, when a passenger fails to pay excess charge on demand and refuses to give name/address, a railway servant may:
options: Apply to a Metropolitan or Judicial Magistrate for recovery
Detain him at the next station
answer: Apply to a Metropolitan or Judicial Magistrate for recovery
explanation: Section 138(4) provides that if a passenger fails or refuses to pay, any railway servant authorised may apply to any Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate of the first or second class for recovery of the sum payable as if it were a fine.

question: Under Section 139, when may a woman or child (unaccompanied by a male passenger) be removed from a higher class carriage?
options: Only at the originating station, or at a junction/terminal/district headquarters station, and only during the day
At any junction station at any time
answer: Only at the originating station, or at a junction/terminal/district headquarters station, and only during the day
explanation: The second proviso to Section 139 protects women and children — they shall not be removed except at the station of commencement of journey or at a junction, terminal station, or station at district headquarters, and only during the day.

question: Under Section 141, the minimum punishment for FIRST offence of pulling alarm chain without reasonable cause is:
answer: Fine of Rs. 500
explanation: The proviso to Section 141 states that for first offence of misusing alarm chain without reasonable cause, punishment shall not be less than a fine of Rs. 500.

question: Under Section 141, the minimum punishment for SECOND or subsequent offence of pulling alarm chain without reasonable cause is:
answer: Imprisonment for 3 months
explanation: The proviso to Section 141(b) provides that for second or subsequent conviction for misusing alarm chain without reasonable cause, punishment shall not be less than imprisonment for three months.

question: The minimum fine for the offence of touting (ticket procuring/supplying business) under Section 143 is:
explanation: The proviso to Section 143(1) provides that in the absence of special reasons, the punishment for touting shall not be less than imprisonment for one month or a fine of Rs. 5,000.

question: Which sections under Chapter XV were decriminalised (imprisonment removed) by the Jan Vishwas Act, 2023?
explanation: The Jan Vishwas (Amendment of Provisions) Act, 2023 (Act 18 of 2023) decriminalised several minor offences under the Railways Act — removing imprisonment for sections 144, 145, 147, 163, 166, 167, and 176.

question: Under Section 150, if malicious endangerment of railway safety is done with intent to cause death and death results, the punishment is:
options: Rigorous imprisonment for not less than 7 years
answer: Death or imprisonment for life
explanation: Section 150(2) provides that if the unlawful act is done with intent to cause death and death results, or with knowledge that the act must in all probability cause death, the punishment is death or imprisonment for life.

question: Under Section 150, the minimum rigorous imprisonment for SECOND or subsequent conviction of maliciously endangering railway safety is:
answer: Not less than 7 years
explanation: The proviso to Section 150(1) provides minimum rigorous imprisonment of: (a) three years for first offence; and (b) seven years for second or subsequent offence.

question: Under Section 153, endangering railway safety by any WILFUL act or omission is punishable with imprisonment up to:
answer: Five years
explanation: Section 153 provides that wilful endangering of railway safety or obstruction of rolling stock is punishable with imprisonment up to five years.

question: Under Section 154, endangering railway safety by a RASH OR NEGLIGENT act is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both
Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both
answer: Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both
explanation: Section 154 distinguishes rash/negligent endangerment from wilful acts — punishable with imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.

question: Under Section 161, 'negligence' in crossing an unmanned level crossing specifically means:
options: Crossing without stopping to observe for approaching rolling stock, OR crossing even while approaching rolling stock is in sight
answer: Crossing without stopping to observe for approaching rolling stock, OR crossing even while approaching rolling stock is in sight
explanation: The Explanation to Section 161 defines negligence as: (a) crossing without stopping to observe whether any approaching rolling stock is in sight; or (b) crossing even while an approaching rolling stock is in sight.

question: Under Section 172, when a railway servant on duty is intoxicated AND it is likely to endanger safety, the punishment is:
options: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine
answer: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
explanation: Section 172 provides two tiers: (a) mere intoxication on duty — fine up to Rs. 500; (b) when intoxication is likely to endanger safety — imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.

question: Under Section 174, obstructing a train by 'rail roko agitation or bandh' is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to Rs. 2,000 or both
answer: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to Rs. 2,000 or both
explanation: Section 174 specifically addresses rail roko, bandh and similar agitations — punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine up to Rs. 2,000 or both.

question: Under Section 175, a railway servant who endangers safety by disobeying any rule or by rash/negligent act is punishable with imprisonment up to:
answer: Two years
explanation: Section 175 provides that a railway servant who while on duty endangers safety by disobeying any rule/instruction or by rash/negligent act shall be punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine up to Rs. 1,000 or both.

question: Under Section 179, persons who commit listed offences may be arrested WITHOUT warrant by:
options: Any railway servant of any rank
Any railway servant OR a police officer not below the rank of a head constable
Only a Station Superintendent or above
answer: Any railway servant OR a police officer not below the rank of a head constable
explanation: Section 179(1) provides that for listed offences, arrest without warrant may be effected by any railway servant or police officer not below the rank of a head constable.

question: A person arrested under Section 179 shall be produced before the nearest Magistrate within:
answer: 24 hours (excluding journey time)
explanation: Section 179(3) provides that a person arrested shall be produced before the nearest Magistrate within twenty-four hours of such arrest, excluding the time necessary for the journey.

question: Sections 180A to 180G (officer authorised inquiry powers) were inserted by:
answer: Railways (Second Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 51 of 2003)
explanation: Sections 180A to 180G were inserted by the Railways (Second Amendment) Act, 2003 (Act 51 of 2003), w.e.f. 1-7-2004.

question: Under Section 181, no court inferior to which court shall try an offence under the Railways Act?
options: Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate of the first class
answer: Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate of the first class
explanation: Section 181 provides that notwithstanding anything in the CrPC, no court inferior to that of a Metropolitan Magistrate or a Judicial Magistrate of the first class shall try an offence under the Act.

question: Under Section 186, the protection of good faith applies to acts done:
options: In pursuance of this Act or any rules or orders made thereunder
Only by the General Manager or above
answer: In pursuance of this Act or any rules or orders made thereunder
explanation: Section 186 provides that no legal proceeding shall lie for anything which is in good faith done or intended to be done in pursuance of this Act or any rules or orders made thereunder.

question: Under Section 188, railway servants are deemed to be public servants for the purposes of:
options: Chapter IX and section 409 of the Indian Penal Code
answer: Chapter IX and section 409 of the Indian Penal Code
explanation: Section 188(1) provides that any railway servant not already a public servant under section 21 IPC shall be deemed to be a public servant for the purposes of Chapter IX and section 409 of the IPC.

question: Chapter IVA on 'Land Acquisition for a Special Railway Project' was inserted by which amendment, w.e.f. which date?
answer: Railways (Amendment) Act, 2008, w.e.f. 31-1-2008
explanation: Chapter IVA containing sections 20A to 20P was inserted by the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2008 (Act 11 of 2008) and is deemed to have come into force on 31st January 2008.

question: Under Section 20D, objections to land acquisition for a special railway project may be filed within:
answer: 30 days from publication of §20A notification
explanation: Section 20D(1) provides that any person interested in the land may, within a period of thirty days from the date of publication of the notification under section 20A, object to the acquisition.

question: After publication of the §20E declaration of acquisition, land vests in:
options: The Central Government free from all encumbrances
The competent authority as a trust
answer: The Central Government free from all encumbrances
explanation: Section 20E(2) provides that on the publication of the declaration, the land shall vest absolutely in the Central Government free from all encumbrances.

question: If no §20E declaration is published within what period after the §20A notification, the notification ceases to have effect?
answer: One year
explanation: Section 20E(3) provides that if no declaration is published within a period of one year from the date of publication of the §20A notification, that notification shall cease to have effect.

question: Under Section 20F(9), the solatium on market value for compulsory acquisition under Chapter IVA is:
explanation: Section 20F(9) provides that in every case, a sum of sixty per centum on the market value shall be awarded, in consideration of the compulsory nature of the acquisition.

question: Under Section 20H, the interest rate awarded on excess arbitration award over the competent authority's award is:
answer: 9% per annum
explanation: Section 20H(5) provides that where the arbitrator's amount exceeds the competent authority's determination, the arbitrator may award interest at nine per cent per annum from the date of taking possession till actual deposit.

question: Under Section 20I, the owner in possession of acquired land must surrender it within how many days of notice?
answer: 60 days
explanation: Section 20I(1) provides that the owner or person in possession must surrender or deliver possession within a period of sixty days of the service of the notice.

question: If acquired land under Chapter IVA remains unutilised for how many years, it reverts to the Central Government?
answer: Five years
explanation: Section 20L(2) provides that when any land acquired under this Act remains unutilised for a period of five years from the date of taking over possession, it shall return to the Central Government by reversion.

question: Under Section 20M, what percentage of profit is shared with original landowners when acquired land is transferred at a higher consideration?
explanation: Section 20M provides that 80% of the difference in the acquisition cost and the consideration received shall be shared amongst the persons from whom the lands were acquired or their heirs.

question: Section 20N explicitly states that which Act shall NOT apply to acquisitions under Chapter IVA?
answer: Land Acquisition Act, 1894
explanation: Section 20N explicitly provides that nothing in the Land Acquisition Act, 1894 shall apply to an acquisition under Chapter IVA.

question: The Railways (Amendment) Act, 2008 received Presidential assent on 28th March 2008 but is deemed to have come into force on:
answer: 31st January 2008
explanation: Section 1(2) of the Railways (Amendment) Act, 2008 provides that it shall be deemed to have come into force on the 31st day of January, 2008 — a retrospective operation.

question: A declaration under Section 20E(1) for acquisition of land for a special railway project shall:
options: Be subject to judicial review by High Courts
Not be called in question in any court or by any other authority
Be appealable before the Railway Rates Tribunal
Require approval of the State Government concerned
answer: Not be called in question in any court or by any other authority
explanation: Section 20E(4) provides that a declaration made by the Central Government shall not be called in question in any court or by any other authority — making it a final and non-justiciable declaration.

question: The Railways Act 1989 has been amended how many times as on 12th August 2025?
answer: Eight times
explanation: As per IndiaCode (as on 12th August 2025), there are 8 amending acts: 1994, 2003 (Second Amendment), 2003 (Amendment), 2005, 2008, Finance Act 2017, Jan Vishwas Act 2023, and 2025.

question: Under Section 103, when the value of a consignment is NOT declared, the liability of the railway administration is:
options: Limited to an amount calculated with reference to the weight of the consignment as prescribed
Determined solely by the Claims Tribunal
answer: Limited to an amount calculated with reference to the weight of the consignment as prescribed
explanation: Section 103(1) provides that when the value is not declared, the liability shall in no case exceed such amount calculated with reference to the weight as may be prescribed.

question: Under Section 100, a railway administration is NOT responsible for loss of hand luggage UNLESS:
options: The luggage weighs more than prescribed limit
Loss was due to negligence or misconduct of railway servants
The passenger holds a first class ticket
answer: Loss was due to negligence or misconduct of railway servants
explanation: Section 100 provides that for luggage carried by the passenger in his charge, the railway administration is not responsible unless it is proved that the loss was due to negligence or misconduct on its part or on the part of any of its servants.

question: Under Section 93, the railway administration is NOT responsible for loss of goods arising from 'natural deterioration or wastage in bulk or weight due to inherent defect, quality or vice of the goods'. This is known as what exception?
answer: Inherent defect exception
explanation: Section 93(g) lists 'natural deterioration or wastage in bulk or weight due to inherent defect, quality or vice of the goods' as one of the excepted causes — the inherent defect exception.

question: Under Section 140, the bond for good behaviour for habitual ticketless travel offenders shall not exceed:
answer: Three years
explanation: Section 140(1) provides that the court may order the convicted person to execute a bond for such period not exceeding three years as it deems fit.

question: Under Section 167, smoking in a railway compartment (when objected to by another passenger) after the Jan Vishwas Act 2023 is punishable with:
answer: Fine not exceeding Rs. 100
explanation: Section 167(3) provides a fine up to Rs. 100. The Jan Vishwas Act, 2023 retained the fine but removed any imprisonment component for smoking.

question: Under Section 176, a railway servant who unnecessarily keeps a level crossing closed against the public is punishable with fine up to:
explanation: Section 176 provides that a railway servant who unnecessarily allows rolling stock to stand across a public road crossing or keeps a level crossing closed against the public shall be punishable with fine up to Rs. 100.

question: Under Section 156, a passenger who persists in travelling on the roof of a carriage after being warned by a railway servant is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to Rs. 500 or both
Imprisonment up to 6 months and fine
Removal from railway only — no criminal penalty
answer: Imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to Rs. 500 or both
explanation: Section 156 provides that a passenger or other person who after being warned persists in travelling on the roof, step or footboard of any carriage or engine shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three months, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both.

question: Under Section 162, a male person who enters a railway carriage reserved for females is punishable with:
options: Fine up to Rs. 500 and forfeiture of pass/ticket
Imprisonment up to 6 months and fine
answer: Fine up to Rs. 500 and forfeiture of pass/ticket
explanation: Section 162 provides that a male person who enters a carriage reserved for females shall, in addition to being liable to forfeiture of his pass or ticket, be punishable with fine up to Rs. 500.

question: Under Section 160, breaking (not merely opening) a level crossing gate is punishable with imprisonment up to:
answer: 5 years
explanation: Section 160(2) provides that breaking any gate or barrier at a level crossing closed to road traffic shall be punishable with imprisonment up to five years — more severe than merely opening it (three years).

question: Under Section 173, a railway servant who abandons his duty before reaching destination without authority is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both
Immediate dismissal and imprisonment up to 5 years
answer: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both
explanation: Section 173 provides that a railway servant who abandons his duty connected with running of a train before reaching destination without authority or without properly handing over to another authorised servant shall be punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine up to Rs. 1,000 or both.

question: Under Section 178, a railway servant who makes a false report in a claims inquiry is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both
answer: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both
explanation: Section 178 provides that a railway servant required to inquire into a compensation claim who makes a false report shall be punishable with imprisonment up to two years, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both.

question: Under Section 177, a railway servant who submits a false return is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both
Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
answer: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both
explanation: Section 177 provides that a railway servant who signs and furnishes a false return shall be punishable with imprisonment up to one year, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both.

question: Under Section 148, a person who makes a false statement in a compensation application under Section 125 is punishable with:
options: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
answer: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
explanation: Section 148 provides that any person who makes a false statement in a compensation application shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both.

question: Under Section 49, a copy of the timetable shall be kept at every station where tickets are issued, in the office of the:
answer: Station Master
explanation: Section 49(2) provides that at every station where tickets are issued to passengers, a copy of the time table in force shall be kept in the office of the station master.

question: Under Section 57, the maximum number of passengers for each compartment shall be exhibited in Hindi, English, and:
options: One or more regional languages commonly in use in the areas served by the railway
The official language of the State concerned
answer: One or more regional languages commonly in use in the areas served by the railway
explanation: Section 57 requires that the maximum number be exhibited in Hindi, English and also in one or more of the regional languages commonly in use in the areas served by the railway.

question: Under Section 55, a person who obtains permission to travel without a ticket shall ordinarily:
options: Get a certificate from the authorised railway servant and subsequently pay the fare
Travel only up to the next junction station
answer: Get a certificate from the authorised railway servant and subsequently pay the fare
explanation: Section 55(2) provides that a person obtaining permission to travel without a ticket shall ordinarily get a certificate from the railway servant that he has been permitted to travel on condition that he subsequently pays the fare payable for the distance to be travelled.
